data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Entergy (ETR) After Its 50% One Year Share Price Jump?
data.description:
• Investors may be wondering whether Entergy at around US$117 a share still offers value, or if most of the easy upside is already on the table.
  • The stock last closed at US$117.44, with returns of 2.2% over 7 days, 12.1% over 30 days, 25.1% year to date and 50.3% over 1 year. These figures naturally raise questions about what is already priced in and how much risk investors are taking on at these levels.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Entergy as a regulated utility with an established role in the US power grid. This has highlighted ongoing interest in essential infrastructure companies and their ability to fund long term capital projects. This backdrop helps frame how investors might be reassessing both the potential and the risks around the stock.
  • Entergy currently holds a 2 out of 6 valuation score. The next step is to look at what different valuation approaches are saying about that pricing and then finish with a broader way to think about what the stock may be worth beyond standard models.

Entergy scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Entergy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today in dollar terms. It is essentially asking what the future stream of cash is worth right now.

For Entergy, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a loss of $1.24b. Analyst and model projections then move through a period of negative FCF in the near term, before turning positive, with projected FCF of $2.88b in 2030. Beyond the explicit analyst window, Simply Wall St extrapolates additional annual FCF estimates up to 2035 using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back and added together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $487.85 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around $117, this implies a 75.9% discount, which indicates that the stock screens as materially undervalued under this particular DCF setup.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Entergy is undervalued by 75.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 64 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Entergy.

Approach 2: Entergy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support that price. It gives a simple shorthand for how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher growth and lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Entergy is trading on a P/E of 30.58x, compared with the Electric Utilities industry average of 22.12x and a peer average of 20.11x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 26.85x for Entergy, which reflects factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for Entergy’s specific fundamentals rather than assuming all utilities deserve the same multiple. Set against the current 30.58x P/E, the 26.85x Fair Ratio suggests the shares are trading above what this framework would consider fair.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.description:

  • The stock last closed at US$260.05, with returns of 8.0% over 7 days, 11.3% over 30 days, 30.1% year to date, 7.0% over 1 year, negative 18.7% over 3 years, and negative 79.9% over 5 years. This mix can change how investors think about both potential and risk.

Boston Beer Company scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Boston Beer Company Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return.

For Boston Beer Company, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $206.2 million. Analysts and extrapolated estimates point to Free Cash Flow of $115 million in 2030, with a series of year by year projections in between that are based on both analyst inputs and Simply Wall St’s own extrapolation beyond the usual 5 year analyst window.

Putting all of those projected cash flows into the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $257.14 per share. Against the recent share price of $260.05, the model suggests the stock is around 1.1% overvalued, which is effectively a small gap.

On this DCF view, Boston Beer Company appears close enough to its calculated fair value that price alone is not a clear signal in either direction.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Boston Beer Company is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Boston Beer Company.

Approach 2: Boston Beer Company Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link the price you pay for a share to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects a mix of growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower, more conservative multiple.

Boston Beer Company currently trades on a P/E of about 24.6x. That sits above the Beverage industry average of roughly 16.9x, but below the peer group average of around 27.2x. To refine this comparison, Simply Wall St uses a “Fair Ratio”, which is the P/E level suggested for Boston Beer Company after accounting for its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio for Boston Beer Company is 13.8x, which sits well below the current P/E of 24.6x. On this Preferred Multiple view, the shares look expensive relative to what the Fair Ratio suggests.

data.title: Canadian Review And FERC Ruling Shape Plains All American Cash Flows
data.description:
• The Canadian Competition Bureau has escalated its review of Keyera's proposed purchase of Plains All American Pipeline's Canadian NGL business, adding more regulatory scrutiny to the transaction.
  • Separately, Plains All American Pipeline received Federal Energy Regulatory Commission approval for its proposed accounting treatment related to the Cactus III pipeline acquisition.
  • These developments affect Plains All American Pipeline's asset mix and the timing of its planned shift toward more fee based income.

NasdaqGS:PAA is trading at $21.885, with the share price up 20.2% year to date and 43.8% over the past year. Over a 5 year period, the stock has returned 244.7%, while the value score currently stands at 4. This gives investors a data point on how the market is pricing the business today.

For readers, a key question is how a prolonged competition review in Canada and the accounting treatment of Cactus III might influence Plains All American Pipeline's cash flow mix and balance sheet over time. These regulatory steps are important markers in the company's push toward more stable, fee based earnings and may shape how investors judge the durability and quality of future results.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Plains All American Pipeline by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Plains All American Pipeline.

Is Plains All American Pipeline's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The Canadian Competition Bureau’s decision to deepen its investigation into Keyera’s purchase of Plains All American Pipeline’s Canadian NGL business introduces timing and deal-completion risk for a transaction that PAA has linked to its plan to lift fee based income from about 80% to 85% and reduce leverage. A prolonged review or potential conditions on the sale could delay when Plains receives sale proceeds and when its asset mix becomes more crude focused, which matters for the coverage of its US$0.4175 quarterly common distribution and its goal of steadier cash flows. By contrast, Federal Energy Regulatory Commission approval of Plains’ proposed accounting for the Cactus III acquisition removes an element of uncertainty. Recording the acquired assets at fair value provides clarity on how the deal will appear on Plains’ books and how future revenues and expenses from Cactus III will flow through reported results, giving investors a clearer view of how that pipeline fits into PAA’s long term, fee based earnings mix.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Plains All American Pipeline Narrative

  • The planned NGL divestiture is central to the narrative of refocusing on U.S. crude oil pipelines. The Competition Bureau’s review directly affects the timing of that shift toward a higher share of fee based income.
  • If the regulator requires remedies that alter pricing or capacity, the expected uplift from redeploying roughly US$3b of proceeds into higher return crude assets could be less straightforward than the narrative suggests.
  • The detailed FERC approval of Cactus III’s accounting treatment gives more transparency on how that acquisition will be reflected in Plains’ results, which is not fully captured in high level narrative assumptions about margin expansion and earnings growth.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Plains All American Pipeline to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Extended scrutiny from the Canadian regulator could delay cash proceeds from the NGL sale, which may slow PAA’s plans to reduce debt and support a dividend that currently sits on tight coverage.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks, including a high level of debt and a dividend that is not well covered by earnings, which could become more pressing if deal timing or terms shift.
  • 🎁 FERC’s approval of Plains’ proposed accounting for the Cactus III acquisition reduces regulatory uncertainty for a major crude pipeline asset and clarifies how it will contribute to reported earnings and cash flow.
  • 🎁 Earnings have grown strongly and the units are described as trading well below an internal fair value estimate, so successful execution on asset sales and Cactus III integration could support the existing income profile and future flexibility.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors should track how quickly the Canadian Competition Bureau progresses its investigation, any conditions attached to Keyera’s purchase of the NGL assets, and whether that affects expected proceeds or the planned increase in fee based income. Updates around leverage, preferred and common distributions, and the contribution of Cactus III to Plains’ cash flows will also be key, especially as peers like Enbridge, Enterprise Products Partners and Kinder Morgan balance similar regulatory and capital allocation pressures. The upcoming earnings release and conference call will be an important checkpoint for management’s latest view on deal timing, capital spending and balance sheet priorities.

data.title: Is Kodiak AI (KDK) Pricing Reflect Future Cash Flow Potential After Recent Share Price Slide
data.description:
• Wondering if Kodiak AI at US$7.85 is a bargain or a value trap? This article walks through what the current price could be saying about the stock.
  • The share price has moved 9.0% over the last 7 days, after a 9.5% decline over 30 days and a 27.5% fall year to date, with the 1 year return at 29.7% in the red.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Kodiak AI as part of broader discussions about listed auto related and AI exposed names, with investors weighing business potential against ongoing volatility in growth oriented sectors. This context helps explain why the stock has seen both short term strength and longer term pressure.
  • Kodiak AI currently has a valuation score of 2 out of 6. The rest of this article breaks down what that means across different valuation methods, while pointing to a more complete way to think about value at the end.

Kodiak AI scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Kodiak AI Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes forecasts of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For Kodiak AI, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $97.47 million. Analyst inputs run out after a few years, so Simply Wall St extends the series using its own assumptions. This is why you see a detailed path of ten year FCF projections. Within these, Kodiak AI’s forecast free cash flow for 2030 is $180 million, with further years continuing on that trajectory according to the model.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$45.98 per share. Against the current share price of US$7.85, the DCF output implies the stock trades at an 82.9% discount. This highlights a wide gap between the modeled value and what the market is currently willing to pay.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Kodiak AI is undervalued by 82.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 63 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Kodiak AI.

Approach 2: Kodiak AI Price vs Book

For companies where earnings are less meaningful or still volatile, price based on book value can be a useful cross check. The P/B ratio compares the market value of the equity to the accounting value of net assets, which many investors use as a rough anchor when profits are not yet a steady guide.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/B usually reflects how fast a business is expected to grow and how risky its cash flows are. Higher growth and stronger perceived durability can support a higher multiple, while more uncertainty typically points to a lower one.

Kodiak AI currently trades on a P/B of 5.06x, compared with an Auto Components industry average of 1.42x and a peer group average of 2.20x. Simply Wall St also uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for P/B, which is the multiple it would expect given factors such as earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio approach aims to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for Kodiak AI’s own characteristics rather than assuming it should trade like the average stock. As the Fair Ratio is not available here, the P/B comparison alone suggests the market is placing a relatively rich value on the current balance sheet.

Result: OVERVALUED

Narratives are introduced here as a simple tool that lets you attach a clear story about Kodiak AI to your numbers, by linking your view of its future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast, then to a fair value that you can compare with today’s price on the Simply Wall St Community page. On that page, Narratives are updated automatically when new results or news arrive. For example, one Kodiak AI Narrative might use a higher fair value such as US$22.0 based on very strong growth assumptions, while another uses a lower fair value such as US$13.0 based on more cautious expectations, giving you a direct way to see how different views on the same company translate into different buy or sell decisions.
